task: I’ve got a workday coming up where I may need to keep moving if the power or internet cuts out for a while. What’s a sensible way to set up my materials and workspace so I can shift to a different spot and keep going without losing track of what I was doing?

turns.agent_response:
Set it up so you can move fast without rebuilding the day.

### Before you start
- save the current files locally
- make a backup copy
- charge laptop, phone, and power bank
- test your hotspot or other backup connection
- keep key contacts and deadlines in one place
- have reference material available offline if you can

### Set up the workspace
- keep the active file easy to find
- use one clear folder for today’s work
- keep chargers and cables together
- put notes, deadlines, and next steps in a single place
- separate “must finish today” from “can wait”

### If power or internet drops
- switch to the device that still works
- use the local copy
- move to the backup connection if needed
- write down the exact stop point before you stop
- keep the next step visible when you come back

### Simple rule
If you can pick up the file, the notes, and the connection plan in one move, you can keep working from somewhere else much more easily.

If you want, I can turn this into a short moving-work checklist.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty solid. I’d want it a little more concrete, though — like a real setup I can use before the day starts.

What I’m after is something like: what goes in the bag, what stays open on the desk, how to mark the last sentence or paragraph I was on, and how to keep the glossary / reference stuff from getting scattered. I’ve had enough of losing ten minutes just re-orienting after a power hiccup.

A short checklist would be useful. Keep it practical.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical pre-start checklist.

### Before the day starts
- save the current file locally
- make a backup copy
- charge laptop, phone, and power bank
- test the hotspot or backup connection
- put charger, cable, and power bank in the bag
- keep glossary / reference docs in one folder or one printed packet
- open the active file and the main reference you’ll need first
- note the last sentence / paragraph you were on
- write the next step in one short line

### Desk setup
- keep only the active file open
- keep today’s reference material together
- keep notes, deadlines, and contact info in one place
- separate “must finish today” from “can wait”

### If power or internet drops
- switch to the device that still works
- use the local copy
- move to the backup connection if needed
- go straight to the saved stop point
- keep the next step visible when you restart

### Simple rule
If you can grab the file, the notes, and the backup connection plan quickly, you can move without losing your place.

If you want, I can make this even shorter as a 5-line version.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good. Still a little generic, though. I wanted something I could actually set up, not just a list of reminders.

Give me the concrete version — like:
- what’s in the bag
- what stays open on the desk
- how you’d mark the exact stop point in the document
- how to keep glossary/reference material from turning into a mess

A 5-line version is fine if it’s specific enough. I don’t need fluff, just a clean setup I can use before I start.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the concrete version.

### 5-line setup
- **In the bag:** laptop charger, phone charger, power bank, cable, notebook, and any printed reference pages you may need.
- **On the desk:** one active file open, one reference folder or packet, and a short note with deadlines/contact info.
- **Stop point:** mark the exact last sentence or paragraph with a highlight, comment, or note like **“resume here”**.
- **Glossary/reference:** keep it in one folder only, or one printed packet only — not split across tabs, downloads, and loose pages.
- **Before you start:** save the file locally, make a backup copy, and write the next step in one line.

### Simple rule
If you can grab the bag, open the file, and see the stop point in one glance, you can move to a different spot without losing time.

If you want, I can make it even tighter into a desk-side checklist.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that works. Specific enough.

The stop-point note and keeping the glossary in one place is the part I wanted. The bag list is fine too — charger, power bank, notebook, printed pages, done.

That’s usable. I can set that up before I start.
